series.title: [v2] hw/riscv/riscv-iommu: Use standard EN_PRI bit for PRI

review.summary: The functional change is small and appears spec-aligned, but it likely introduces a guest-visible compatibility regression by dropping support for the previously exposed custom TC[32] PRI-enable bit with no compatibility handling.

stages.A: Patch intent is clear: replace a temporary custom PRI enable bit with the architectural EN_PRI bit.
stages.B: Local command-based inspection was not possible in this environment, so the review is based on the provided diff and QEMU compatibility expectations for guest-visible register semantics.
stages.C: The code change itself is mechanically correct and improves readability by using a named architectural bit definition.
stages.D: Main risk: existing guests or migration streams that relied on TC[32] to enable PRI will stop working after this change.
stages.E: Recommendation: preserve backward compatibility by accepting both bits, or by wiring old behavior behind a compat knob/machine-type compatibility path.

findings.title: Dropping the legacy PRI-enable bit changes guest-visible behavior
findings.description: Previous QEMU behavior enabled automatic page-request generation when TC[32] was set. This patch removes that behavior entirely and requires RISCV_IOMMU_DC_TC_EN_PRI instead. If any existing guest software, tests, or migration scenarios depend on the legacy custom bit, PRI will silently stop working after upgrade. In QEMU, even temporary device-register behavior can become part of the effective guest ABI once released, so replacing it without a compatibility path is risky.
patch_context: -     * TC[32] is reserved for custom extensions, used here to temporarily
-     * enable automatic page-request generation for ATS queries.
findings.suggestion: Keep the architectural EN_PRI bit as the preferred path, but also honor the legacy TC[32] bit for compatibility, or restrict the behavior change behind a compat property / machine-version check so existing guests and migration pairs do not regress.
findings.confidence_reason: The regression follows directly from the diff, but confirming whether the old TC[32] behavior is already exposed in released machine types would require inspecting the surrounding QEMU tree and compatibility policies.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
